paper_title: DiagramBank: A Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent advances in autonomous ``AI scientist'' systems have demonstrated the ability to automatically write scientific manuscripts and codes with execution. However, producing a publication-grade scientific diagram (e.g., teaser figure) is still a major bottleneck in the ``end-to-end'' paper generation process. For example, a teaser figure acts as a strategic visual interface and serves a different purpose than derivative data plots. It demands conceptual synthesis and planning to translate complex logic workflow into a compelling graphic that guides intuition and sparks curiosity. Existing AI scientist systems usually omit this component or fall back to an inferior alternative. To bridge this gap, we present DiagramBank, a large-scale dataset consisting of 89,422 schematic diagrams curated from existing top-tier scientific publications, designed for multimodal retrieval and exemplar-driven scientific figure generation. DiagramBank is developed through our automated curation pipeline that extracts figures and corresponding in-text references, and uses a CLIP-based filter to differentiate schematic diagrams from standard plots or natural images. Each instance is paired with rich context from abstract, caption, to figure-reference pairs, enabling information retrieval under different query granularities. We release DiagramBank in a ready-to-index format and provide a retrieval-augmented generation codebase to demonstrate exemplar-conditioned synthesis of teaser figures. DiagramBank is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/zhangt20/DiagramBank with code at https://github.com/csml-rpi/DiagramBank.

[ 1 7 5 8 0 2 . 4 0 6 2 : r DiagramBank: Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Tingwen Zhang zhangt20@rpi.edu Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute Troy, New York, USA Zhen Xu zach.xu@uchicago.edu University of Chicago Chicago, Illinois, USA Ling Yue yuel2@rpi.edu Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute Troy, New York, USA Shaowu Pan pans2@rpi.edu Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute Troy, New York, USA Abstract Recent advances in autonomous AI scientist systems have demonstrated the ability to automatically write scientific manuscripts and codes with execution. However, producing publication-grade scientific diagram (e.g., teaser figure) is still major bottleneck in the end-to-end paper generation process. For example, teaser figure acts as strategic visual interface and serves different purpose than derivative data plots. It demands conceptual synthesis and planning to translate complex logic workflow into compelling graphic that guides intuition and sparks curiosity. Existing AI scientist systems usually omit this component or fall back to an inferior alternative. To bridge this gap, we present DiagramBank, largescale dataset consisting of 89,422 schematic diagrams curated from existing top-tier scientific publications, designed for multimodal retrieval and exemplar-driven scientific figure generation. DiagramBank is developed through our automated curation pipeline that extracts figures and corresponding in-text references, and uses CLIPbased filter to differentiate schematic diagrams from standard plots or natural images. Each instance is paired with rich context from abstract, caption, to figure-reference pairs, enabling information retrieval under different query granularities. We release DiagramBank in ready-to-index format and provide retrieval-augmented generation codebase to demonstrate exemplar-conditioned synthesis of teaser figures. DiagramBank is publicly available at https:// huggingface.co/datasets/zhangt20/DiagramBank with code at https: //github.com/csml-rpi/DiagramBank. Keywords Scientific diagrams, Dataset curation, Diagram retrieval, Retrievalaugmented generation, Multimodal generation

sections.title:
1 Introduction
The field of autonomous “AI scientist” systems is rapidly evolv-
ing, with recent advances [13, 26] demonstrating agents capable of
writing code, conducting experiments, and even drafting coherent
manuscripts with increasing autonomy. Despite these advances,
end-to-end paper generation still remains noticeably incomplete in
one of the most visible places: the teaser figure, which is often a
sections.content: Both authors contributed equally to this research. Corresponding author. method overview diagram on the first page. Unlike standard plots, which follow routine generation pipeline from data, scientific diagrams require cross-modal abstraction and strategic layout planning [1, 29]. Previous work also shows that authors often identify central figure that serves as de-facto graphical abstract [25]. To illustrate, good teaser figure should accomplish three goals: convey the papers core contribution at glance, pique the readers curiosity, and faithfully represent its key findings without overstatement. In contemporary data mining and machine learning venues, the teaser figure is often the most efficient carrier of papers core workflow, highlighting the objects, modules, and information flow that reader needs to build mental model of the method. When automated pipelines omit this figure or replace it with inferior alternatives, the resulting paper cannot attract readers or persuade peers, let alone become well-circulated scientific artifact [25]. Overall, good scientific diagram compresses long-form technical description into structured visual program: (i) selecting salient entities and operations, (ii) assigning them to spatial regions, (iii) routing relations (arrows/edges) without clutter, and (iv) rendering legible labels and legends. This structure-rich, symbolic nature distinguishes diagrams from natural images and is key reason that diagram understanding has been studied as separate problem from standard vision tasks [9]. Unfortunately, standard text-to-image models fail to generate scientific schematic diagrams that are dense compositions of many semantically linked objects, consistent arrow topology, and readable text [3]. Recent research has begun to address these issues via explicit planning or intermediate representations (e.g., layout-guided generation or programmatic diagram specifications) [15, 23, 27]. However, even with better planners, models still need to be exposed to high-quality design exemplars to learn the visual conventions of scientific communication (e.g., module grouping, caption-figure alignment, and venue-specific aesthetics). This motivates complementary direction: building curated exemplar bank that can be retrieved and used during authoring. There is large body of work on mining and understanding scientific figures, but existing datasets are not designed for diagram design and teaser authoring. Beyond figure mining and classification, recent work has also studied visual summary identification in scientific papers, aiming to select representative figure , , Zhang et al. Figure 1: DiagramBank-RAG framework (self-generated example). The above workflow diagram is automatically generated by our developed RAG framework: we retrieve exemplars from DiagramBank via our RAG pipeline and use Nano Banana 3 Pro to synthesize the final schematic. The figure, therefore, serves both as an overview of the system components, paper ingestion and figure/context extraction, DiagramBank indexing, retrieval-augmented prompt construction, and generation with downstream judging, and as concrete demonstration that our dataset and retrieval module can directly support producing paper-ready teaser-style diagrams. (e.g., central figure or graphical-abstract-like summary) given the paper content. For example, Yamamoto et al. [24] propose self-supervised approach that leverages figure references in the body text to train model for ranking figures as visual summaries, highlighting both the demand for such representative figures and the challenge of obtaining domain-specific labels at scale. Figurecaption corpora such as SciCap [5] support caption generation and figure understanding at scale, yet they are dominated by heterogeneous figure types and are primarily optimized for language supervision rather than exemplar-driven visual authoring. On the classification side, DocFigure [7] provides labeled scientific figures to enable figure-type recognition, but it treat diagrams as one of many categories and do not preserve the paper context that explains why the figure is structured in particular way. ACL-Fig [8] provides scientific figures and tables from ACL Anthology, but missed out on other venues like ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS, and TMLR. Meanwhile, diagram-centric datasets such as AI2D [9] provide dense, element-level annotations for educational diagrams, but their domain (grade-school science) and distribution differ substantially from the schematic conventions of modern AI/ML papers. Relatedly, recent work has begun to frame scientific multimodal summarization with multimodal output as joint task of generating textual summary while selecting representative image (e.g., graphical abstract) for each paper [28]. Finally, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become standard paradigm for grounding model outputs with external evidence [11], but applying RAG to diagrams requires (i) high-quality, license-aware corpus, and (ii) rich textual metadata so that retrieval can be conditioned at different granularities (paper-level topic vs. figure-level content). To our knowledge, there is no public, large-scale dataset that simultaneously offers (a) high-quality schematic diagrams, (b) paper metadata for domainaware retrieval, and (c) figure-local context spans that connect diagram to the surrounding scientific narrative. We present DiagramBank, large-scale dataset of 89,422 schematic diagrams mined from top-tier AI/ML venues with accompanying paper metadata, built explicitly to support multimodal retrieval and exemplar-driven teaser-style figure generation. Each dataset instance is self-contained record that couples diagram image with multiple layers of text context: paper title and abstract (coarsegrained intent), figure caption (fine-grained semantics), and figurereference spans extracted from the body (how authors operationalize and explain the visual). This design enables retrieval under different query granularities, which is critical in practice: users may start from paper-level description (what is my paper about?) and progressively refine toward figure-level target (what should my overview diagram look like?). DiagramBank is intended as an infrastructure for both the data mining community and the emerging line of scientific authoring systems. Concretely, we make the following contributions: large-scale, retrieval-ready diagram dataset. We release DiagramBank with 89,422 schematic diagrams paired with paper metadata and figure-local context, in format ready for indexing and downstream retrieval. DiagramBank: Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation , , Contextual enrichment for controllable retrieval. Each diagram is linked to paper-level (title/abstract) and figurelevel (caption and in-text reference spans) signals, enabling hierarchical retrieval that reduces domain drift and improves specificity. Baselines for retrieval and retrieval-augmented diagram authoring. We provide baseline code for retrieval, and RAG-style exemplar conditioning pipeline to demonstrate how retrieved design exemplars can guide teaser-style diagram synthesis.

sections.title:
2 Dataset Construction
The design of DiagramBank follows a four-stage pipeline: meta-
data acquisition, content extraction, schematic classification, and
relational aggregation.
sections.content: Data Acquisition. We source our corpus from the OpenReview platform, targeting four top-tier machine learning venues: ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS, and TMLR, spanning the years 2017 to 2025. Using the OpenReview Python API [18], we collect the PDF files and comprehensive metadata for candidate papers. The extracted paper-level attributes are stored in Papers relation and include bibliographic details (title, abstract, authors, creation date), semantic content (keywords, primary subject areas, TL;DR summaries), and platform-specific metadata (reviewer scores, decision status, URLs, and unique platform IDs). The Bibtex of every paper is also included to attribute the original work. Figure and Context Extraction. To process the visual content, we employ PDFFigures 2.0 [4] to parse the downloaded PDFs, extracting all figures and their corresponding captions. While PDFFigures 2.0 identifies both tables and figures, we discard table artifacts to focus strictly on visual illustrations. Beyond the image and caption, capturing the semantic context in which figure appears is crucial for understanding its scientific intent. We utilize PyMuPDF [2] to parse the text blocks of every PDF, extracting all paragraphs that explicitly cite specific figure number. This text forms the figure_context attribute, linking the visual representation to the authors argumentative flow. Schematic Classification. core challenge in constructing DiagramBank is distinguishing explanatory diagrams (e.g., system architectures, flowcharts) from data visualizations (e.g., bar charts, scatter plots) and natural images. We address this by implementing filtering pipeline using Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training (CLIP) [21]. Specifically, we utilize the ViT-B-32 model from OpenCLIP [6] to classify extracted figures into discrete categories: diagram, plot, image, or other. Only figures classified as diagrams with confidence score exceeding 0.85 are retained. These validated entries are stored in Figures relation, which contains the figure path, caption, extracted context, classification type, and confidence score. Data Aggregation and Filtering. We manage the relational data using DuckDB [20]. To construct the primary dataset, we first execute an inner join between the Papers and Figures relations on the unique platform_id. The resulting schema is deliberately denormalized, repeating paper-level metadata (e.g., abstract, title) for every figure record. This redundancy ensures that every retrieved exemplar in DiagramBank is self-contained unit, providing all necessary semantic and structural context for retrieval-augmented generation without requiring complex runtime joins. Following aggregation, we apply strict two-stage filtering pipeline to ensure high data quality. First, we filter based on peerreview outcomes, retaining only figures belonging to papers with positive decisions (e.g., Accept, Spotlight, Oral, or Poster). Second, to isolate explanatory diagrams from statistical plots or natural images, we utilize zero-shot CLIP-based classification pipeline. We define the classification confidence as the softmax probability derived from the cosine similarity between the image embedding and the text embeddings of our four target classes (diagram, plot, photo, other). Through manual inspection of the classification results, we established confidence threshold of 0.85. We retain only figures classified as diagram that exceed this threshold, ensuring the dataset contains high-fidelity schematic representations while minimizing false positives.

sections.title:
3.1 Scale and Subset Sizes
DiagramBank is derived from a large pool of non-table figures
extracted from OpenReview PDFs. Across all processed venues
and years, we extract 452,339 non-table figures in total (plots, dia-
grams, photos, and others), of which 89,422 (19.8%) are classified as
diagrams by our CLIP-based type classifier (Table 2). Because auto-
matic filtering inevitably introduces a precision–recall trade-off, we
release the full diagram set together with the CLIP confidence score
(clip_confidence) to enable application-specific thresholding.
sections.content: To support common usage patterns, we report two practical subsets: (1) an all-confidence subset that includes all figures labeled as diagram by the classifier (no confidence threshold), and (2) highconfidence subset obtained by thresholding clip_confidence (here shown with > 0.85), which improves precision for retrieval and exemplar-driven generation. In addition, when paper decision metadata is available, we report counts for an accepted-paper subset (e.g., Accept/Poster/Spotlight/Oral), which is often preferred for studying publication-grade design conventions. Overall, confidence thresholding reduces the diagram pool from 89,422 to 59,765 (about one third fewer instances), which can be beneficial for precision-sensitive retrieval and for avoiding nonschematic false positives in RAG pipelines. Meanwhile, Figure 2(b) shows that the dataset volume grows sharply in recent years, consistent with the rapid increase in AI/ML publication output on OpenReview. , , Zhang et al. (a) Caption Verbosity Trend (b) Dataset Growth (20172025) (c) Top Subject Areas Figure 2: Statistical Overview of the Dataset. (a) The average caption length (number of words) has steadily decreased from 40 words in 2017 to 35 words in 2025, suggesting shift toward more concise descriptions or an increase in supplementary figures. (b) The volume of extracted figures shows exponential growth, with the 20232025 period accounting for the majority of the data, reflecting the surge in AI research output. (c) Distribution of figures across top subject areas reveals distinct venue-specific taxonomies; for instance, generative models is heavily represented by ICLR, while deep learning is dominated by NeurIPS, highlighting the need for cross-venue keyword normalization. Accepted subset Venue All papers High-confidence diagrams (clip_confidence > 0.85) 31,325 12,550 ICLR 8,045 8,005 ICML 14,226 13,533 NeurIPS 6,169 3,849 TMLR Total 37,937 All-confidence diagrams (no threshold) 19,261 12,204 20,582 5, ICLR ICML NeurIPS TMLR Total 57,808 59,765 46,460 12,267 21,569 9,126 89, Table 1: DiagramBank subset sizes under two filtering regimes: (i) all diagrams predicted by the CLIP classifier and (ii) high-confidence subset obtained by thresholding clip_confidence. We also report an accepted-paper subset when decision metadata is available.

sections.title:
3.2 Figure Type Distribution
Table 2 reports the composition of the full extracted non-table figure
corpus by venue. Across all venues, plots dominate the visual con-
tent (65.2%), while diagrams account for 19.8% and photos for 11.5%.
The diagram fraction is relatively stable across venues (roughly
∼18–21%), indicating that schematic figures are a substantial and
recurring component of modern AI/ML papers.
sections.content: We also report the mean confidence score per predicted type. Plots tend to receive the highest confidence on average, while diagrams have lower (but still strong) mean confidence, reflecting the broader stylistic diversity of schematic figures (e.g., flowcharts, architectures, conceptual maps) compared to canonical chart types. This observation motivates exposing clip_confidence in the release so that users can tighten or relax filtering depending on their tolerance for noise. Venue Figure Type Count Rel. % Avg. Conf. Rel. % is computed within each venue. Avg. Conf. is the mean clip_confidence for the assigned label.

sections.title:
3.3 Visual Density and Caption Characteristics
Table 3 characterizes venue-level visual density using three indica-
tors: figures per paper, visual breadth, and average caption length.
sections.content: DiagramBank: Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation , , Two patterns are particularly relevant for retrieval and diagram authoring. First, visual breadth is consistently high (79.897.6%), meaning that most papers contain at least one extractable figure. This is important for retrieval because it ensures dense coverage of the literature and reduces the number of empty papers during indexing. Second, there are clear venue-level differences in figure density and caption verbosity. ICLR exhibits the lowest figure density (4.79 figures/paper) and shortest captions on average, while TMLR contains the richest visual content (9.22 figures/paper) and the longest captions. These differences imply that retrieval system must handle both sparse and visually dense papers; in particular, high figure density increases the number of within-paper candidates, strengthening the case for coarse-to-fine (paper-to-figure) retrieval such as our hierarchical indexing strategy (Section 4). Finally, Figure 2(a) complements these venue-level statistics by showing mild trend toward shorter captions over time, while Figure 2(c) highlights that keyword/subject-area distributions vary across venues (e.g., venue-specific taxonomies), motivating normalization and multi-stage filtering for domain-aligned retrieval. Venue ICLR ICML NeurIPS TMLR Figs / Paper Visual Breadth (%) Avg. Caption Words 4.79 8.46 6.68 9. 79.8 95.2 94.1 97.6 36.1 42.2 44.0 45.3 Table 3: Visual density statistics per venue. Visual breadth is the percentage of papers containing at least one extracted non-table figure.

sections.title: 3.4 Release and Documentation
sections.content: Release structure and schema. DiagramBank is distributed via the HuggingFace Datasets interface and can be consumed either (i) as single table of denormalized records or (ii) via optional filtered subsets (Section A). Table 4 summarizes the main fields. Field names in the public release follow consistent naming convention and are documented in the dataset card. Documentation and usability. Beyond the main paper, we include dataset card/datasheet (Appendix A) that specifies composition, collection, intended use, known limitations, and recommended evaluation protocols. We also provide baseline indexing and retrieval code to make the dataset immediately usable for retrievalaugmented generation and related benchmarking. single caption-only vector search is often unreliable: generic phrases (e.g., framework overview) appear across many fields, causing domain drift, and lexical overlap alone does not guarantee matching paper context. We therefore use hierarchical retrieval strategy that first matches paper-level intent, then refines to figurelevel semantics.

sections.title:
4.1 Multi-Granularity Index Construction
Each diagram is paired with multi-level text: paper title, paper
abstract, and figure caption. We build three separate indices rather
than mixing all text into one embedding:
sections.content: Title Index for coarse topical/domain filtering. Abstract Index for problem setting and methodology matching. Caption Index for figure-level semantic matching (modules, components, relations). We compute embeddings with an off-the-shelf text embedding model (OpenAI text-embedding-3-* in our implementation [17]) and store indices in FAISS for fast ANN search. All three indices are loaded once and reused across stages (Figure 3).

sections.title:
4.2 Hierarchical Three-Stage Retrieval
Let the query be 𝑞 = (𝑞title, 𝑞abs, 𝑞cap). Retrieval proceeds from broad
to specific:
sections.content: Stage 1: Title filtering. Query the Title Index with 𝑞title to get candidate papers 𝑆1 = TopKtitle (𝑞title, 𝑘 = 𝑡1), typically 𝑡1 [102, 103], which removes most cross-domain noise. Stage 2: Abstract refinement (Deep Fetch). Within 𝑆1, query the Abstract Index with 𝑞abs to obtain 𝑆2 = TopKabs (𝑞abs paper 𝑆1, 𝑘 = 𝑡2), with 𝑡2 102, ensuring papers match beyond topic keywords. Because ANN systems may lose recall when filtering, we use Deep Fetch: retrieve larger pool from the full index, then filter by 𝑆1 and keep the best 𝑡2: fetch𝑘 = 𝑆1 𝛼 . Stage 3: Caption matching (Deep Fetch). Finally, query the Caption Index with 𝑞cap and restrict to 𝑆2 to retrieve the final exemplars: figs = TopKcap (𝑞cap paper 𝑆2, 𝑘 = 𝐾), again using Deep Fetch fetch𝑘 = 𝑆2 𝛽,

sections.title:
4 System Overview: DiagramBank-RAG
DiagramBank-RAG is a retrieval module for exemplar-driven sci-
entific diagram authoring. Given a user’s paper title/abstract and
an intended figure caption (or detailed description), it retrieves
schematic exemplars from DiagramBank that are both domain-
aligned and content-aligned. The retrieved diagrams serve as visual
priors for a RAG pipeline, guiding layout, grouping, iconography,
and style.
sections.content: where 𝛽 is larger since each paper may contain multiple figures. The output is small set of exemplars (e.g., 𝐾 = 3) with captions and paper metadata, which are injected into downstream generation prompts. Overall, DiagramBank-RAG provides simple retrieval backbone for exemplar-conditioned diagram generation by enforcing paper relevance first and figure specificity last, improving both layout plausibility and adherence to scientific diagram conventions. , , Field platform_id venue year paper_title paper_abstract paper_authors paper_keywords paper_subject areas paper_tldr paper_decision paper_scores paper_url paper_bibtex figure_id figure_path caption figure_context clip_label clip_confidence Zhang et al. Type string string int string string string/list string/list string/list string string float/list string string string/int string string string string float Description Unique OpenReview identifier used to join paperand figure-level information. One of {ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS, TMLR}. Publication year (20172025 in this release). Paper title. Paper abstract. Author string or list (as available from OpenReview metadata). Keywords (when available). Primary subject areas / taxonomy tags (when available). TL;DR summary (when available). Decision status (when available). Aggregated reviewer scores (when available). URL to the paper on OpenReview (and/or PDF URL). BibTeX entry for attribution to the original work. Figure identifier within the source PDF (e.g., figure number). Relative path to the extracted figure image in the release. Figure caption extracted by PDFFigures 2.0. Paragraphs that cite the figure number, extracted from the PDF text. CLIP-based type label (e.g., diagram, plot, photo, other). Confidence score for the CLIP-based classification. Table 4: DiagramBank record schema (denormalized). Exact field names are documented in the dataset card and repository. Figure 3: Hierarchical Three-Stage Retrieval Pipeline. The system uses coarse-to-fine filtering strategy to ensure domain relevance and figure-level specificity. Stage 1 retrieves candidate papers using title index to constrain the search space to the target discipline (𝑆1). Stage 2 reranks within 𝑆1 using an abstract index to retain papers with similar problem settings and methodology (𝑆2), aided by Deep Fetch mechanism to preserve recall under filtering. Stage 3 performs caption-level matching within 𝑆2 to retrieve the final figure exemplars aligned with the users intended diagram description.

sections.title:
5 Case Study
To demonstrate the efficacy of our retrieval-augmented generation
approach, we present a case study on visualizing the “Code2MCP”
framework [19]. We compare the output of a baseline model (prompt-
only) against our RAG-enhanced pipeline.
sections.content: The Baseline Limitation. Without access to visual priors, the baseline model relies entirely on the textual prompt to determine layout and style. As seen in Figure 4(a), the resulting image adopts generic DMemphis aestheticcharacterized by high-contrast primary colors (dark blue, bright orange, and green) and linear, DiagramBank: Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation , , (a) Baseline (No RAG) Generic "DMemphis" style with high-contrast colors. (b) Ours (With RAG) Adopts the palette and layout of the retrieved references. Figure 4: Impact of Retrieval. Comparing the baseline generation (a) against our RAG-augmented approach (b). The RAG model successfully transfers visual traits from the references in Figure 5, while the baseline does not preserve the low-level visual details. blocky layout. While the diagram accurately captures the topological nodes of the workflow (e.g., GitHub Repos to MCP Tools), it lacks the refined aesthetic typical of high-quality academic publications. Furthermore, relying solely on text prompts forces the user to spend valuable token length describing visual constraints rather than focusing on the semantic content of the system. The Retrieval Advantage. Our system queries the DiagramBank dataset to retrieve visual exemplars relevant to the input query. Figure 4b displays the top-3 retrieved references. The system identifies Reference Style 1 (a unifying code CoT diagram) as strong stylistic match. This reference features sophisticated pastel color palette, rounded grouping containers for sub-processes, and cyclical central workflow. Visual Transfer and Synthesis. The RAG-augmented output, shown in Figure 4(b), demonstrates successful transfer of visual traits from the retrieved references. Unlike the baseline: Stylistic Consistency: The model abandons the harsh highcontrast colors of the baseline in favor of the professional pastel palette (light grays, soft blues, and greens) observed in Reference Style 1. Structural Coherence: The layout shifts from simple linear progression to more complex, nested structure. The central Code2MCP Process is rendered as cyclical multiagent workflow (Code Analysis Environment Setup Tool Function Design), mirroring the cyclical motifs found in the retrieved exemplars. Iconography: The system adopts specific visual cues, such as the folder icons for repositories and the gear icons for services, creating diagram that feels bespoke rather than templated. This comparison validates that retrieving relevant visual context allows the generation model to produce diagrams that are not only semantically accurate but also stylistically aligned with existing scientific literature. (a) Reference Style 1 [12] (b) Reference Style 2 [14] (c) Reference Style 3 [22] Figure 5: Retrieved References. The top three retrieved exemplars from DiagramBank used to guide the generation process. , , Zhang et al.

sections.title:
6 Limitations and Future Work
DiagramBank relies on automatic heuristics for filtering and con-
text extraction, which may introduce label noise and incomplete
reference spans. Retrieval can return stylistically or logically mis-
matched exemplars, and such errors may propagate in RAG-based
generation. Moreover, current image generators still struggle with
dense arrow topology and legible text, limiting “paper-ready” fi-
delity without post-editing. This limitation is consistent with recent
benchmark studies showing that state-of-the-art text-to-image mod-
els still struggle to render specialized structured text (e.g., equations,
code, flowcharts) accurately and consistently [3].
sections.content: This limitation is also reflected in recent studies on automated graphical abstract generation via prompt engineering, which report incoherent elements and inconsistent visual quality in AI-generated outputs [16]. More broadly, the scientific publishing community has highlighted risks of detail-level errors and automation bias when using generative AI for scientific illustrations [10]. Future work includes stronger discriminators/rerankers for exemplar selection and the use of explicit intermediate representations (e.g., layout programs or vector-graphics specifications) for controllable, editable diagram synthesis. Coverage is also constrained by openaccess availability, which may induce sampling bias and reduce cross-domain diversity.

sections.title:
7 Conclusion
This paper targets a practical but under-served bottleneck in end-
to-end autonomous paper generation: producing a teaser-style
schematic that clearly communicates a method’s global workflow.
To bridge text-centric writing pipelines and figure-centric scientific
communication, we introduced DiagramBank, a retrieval-oriented
corpus of 89,422 schematic diagrams mined from top-tier AI/ML
publications. DiagramBank is built via an automated pipeline that
extracts figures, gathers multi-level textual grounding (title/ab-
stract, caption, and in-text reference spans), and applies a CLIP-
based filter to separate schematics from plots and natural images.
By packaging each diagram with both paper-level intent and figure-
local narrative evidence, the dataset supports retrieval at different
query granularities and enables exemplar-driven authoring. We
release DiagramBank in a ready-to-index format with baseline
retrieval and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) code, showing
how exemplars can improve the plausibility of layout, grouping,
and information flow in teaser-like diagrams.
sections.content: Ethics and Fairness DiagramBank supports multimodal retrieval and exemplar-driven scientific figure authoring. Because it is derived from real publications and can be used in generative pipelines, we summarize key ethical, privacy, fairness, and misuse considerations. Provenance, permissions, and redistribution. DiagramBank is mined from programmatically accessible OpenReview PDFs for four AI/ML venues from 20172025, and we preserve source identifiers/URLs for attribution and traceability. Figures originate from third-party papers and may be subject to copyright or license terms; the dataset is intended for research and educational use, and users should cite and comply with the original works terms. We will honor reasonable takedown requests from rights holders. Privacy. We do not intentionally collect sensitive personal data. Metadata is publicly available and included only for bibliographic attribution and retrieval context. Since some figures may contain identifying elements, we provide figure-level metadata for auditing and recommend filtering/removing suspicious items; we discourage privacy-invasive use. Fairness and bias. DiagramBank reflects the AI/ML publication ecosystem and may exhibit domain, temporal, and availability biases (OpenReview coverage). If an accepted-paper subset is used, it may further amplify venue-specific norms; we therefore encourage reporting results on both all-paper and accepted-only subsets when relevant. We do not claim demographic representativeness. Misuse. The dataset can enable generation of paper-style diagrams, which may be misused to fabricate or mislead. We recommend clear disclosure for generated figures where appropriate, provenance tracking to retrieved exemplars, and human verification prior to publication. Recent discussions in scientific publishing also caution that AI-generated illustrations can appear convincing while containing subtle inaccuracies, underscoring the need for transparency and careful human review [10]. Human subjects. DiagramBank is built from publicly available scholarly documents without interacting with human participants or collecting non-public personal information; we thus do not treat it as human-subjects research, while maintaining responsible documentation and takedown procedures. Generative AI Use Disclosure We used generative AI tools in two limited ways: 1) We used an AIbased writing assistant to improve the grammar and clarity of the manuscript. All technical content, claims, and experimental descriptions were written and verified by the authors. 2) Figure 1 and Figure 3 are self-generated example: after retrieving exemplars from DiagramBank via our retrieval-augmented generation pipeline, we used Nano Banana 3 Pro to synthesize the final schematic. The figure is intended as an illustrative system overview rather than ground-truth diagram from the dataset. No generative AI tool was used to create or modify the underlying DiagramBank records (PDF ingestion, figure extraction, caption/context extraction, and CLIP-based filtering), except insofar as standard pretrained models (e.g., CLIP) are part of the automated filtering pipeline described in the paper. Dataset Card / Datasheet for DiagramBank A.1 Dataset summary Name: DiagramBank Modalities: image (diagram) + text (caption, in-text context) + paper metadata (title/abstract/keywords/etc.) Task types supported: multimodal retrieval, retrieval benchmarking, exemplar-conditioned diagram authoring (RAG), diagram style/structure analysis Time span: 20172025 Source venues: ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS, TMLR (from OpenReview) A.2 Motivation and intended use DiagramBank targets practical bottleneck in scientific authoring: producing high-quality teaser-style workflow diagrams. The dataset is intended to: (i) enable retrieval of semantically relevant diagram exemplars at multiple granularities (paper-/caption-/context-level), (ii) support benchmarking of multimodal retrieval methods on schematic figures, and (iii) serve as reference material for exemplar-driven figure authoring systems. It is not intended for identity inference or for producing misleading scientific artifacts. A.3 Dataset composition Each instance corresponds to single extracted figure that is classified as diagram by CLIP-based filter and is enriched with: Figure-level content: diagram image, caption, and in-text reference context (paragraphs citing the figure). Paper-level context: title, abstract, and additional OpenReview metadata (when available), repeated per figure to make each record self-contained. Subset sizes (diagrams only). We provide multiple optional subsets to support different precision/coverage needs: A.4 Data sources and collection process We collect candidate papers and metadata from OpenReview using the OpenReview API. We download the PDFs and parse figures using PDFFigures 2.0, extracting images and captions. We then extract figure reference context by parsing PDF text blocks and selecting paragraphs that explicitly cite figure number. A.5 Filtering, labeling, and quality control Figure-type labeling. We apply CLIP-based zero-shot classifier (OpenCLIP ViT-B-32) to label each extracted figure into diagram, plot, photo, or other. The public release focuses on figures labeled as diagram; we store clip_confidence to support controlled filtering. Quality control signals. Because extraction is automated, DiagramBank provides multiple signals for auditing and filtering: caption text, in-text context spans, venue/year identifiers, and confidence scores. We encourage users to apply additional application-specific checks (e.g., removing diagrams with missing/short captions or extremely small images) depending on their tasks. A.6 Recommended splits and evaluation protocols DiagramBank is primarily designed for retrieval, so fixed train/validation/test split is not required. For benchmarking, we recommend: Temporal split: train on earlier years and test on later years (e.g., train: 20172023; dev: 2024; test: 2025) to reduce near-duplicate leakage across revisions. Venue split: evaluate generalization by holding out one venue (e.g., train on three venues, test on the fourth). Confidence-controlled evaluation: report results on both all-confidence and high-confidence subsets. A.7 Distribution and access DiagramBank is hosted on HuggingFace Datasets, with accompanying code in public GitHub repository. The dataset card and repository documentation describe how to download, load, and index the dataset, and provide baseline retrieval and RAG pipelines for reproducible experimentation. A.8 Licensing and attribution DiagramBank includes content mined from third-party publications. Rights to figures remain with the original authors and/or publishers, subject to the license and terms under which the source papers are distributed. We include BibTeX and source URLs for attribution and request that users cite both this paper and the original papers when using specific diagrams. The license for the dataset packaging and accompanying code is specified in the public repository. DiagramBank: Large-scale Dataset of Diagram Design Exemplars with Paper Metadata for Retrieval-Augmented Generation , , A.9 Ethical considerations and potential risks DiagramBank inherits biases from the OpenReview ecosystem and AI/ML venue conventions (domain, temporal, and selection biases). The dataset may also be used in generative pipelines, raising misuse risks (e.g., fabrication or misleading figures). We recommend provenance tracking, clear disclosure when figures are AI-generated, and human verification before any real-world deployment or publication use. We will respond to reasonable takedown requests from rights holders. A.10 Known limitations Heuristic noise. Automated extraction can yield imperfect captions or incomplete context spans. Classification errors. CLIP filtering may produce false positives/negatives, especially for atypical diagram styles. Coverage constraints. The dataset scope is limited to accessible OpenReview venues and the years considered. Supply Problem ( Tool Creation ) :** , , Zhang et al. - Background : Light red / pink gradient to indicate the bottleneck area - Title at top : " SUPPLY BOTTLENECK " in bold red text - Main visual : large GitHub repository icon ( folder with GitHub logo ) at the top left - Below it : Multiple scattered code file icons ( document symbols with " </ >" text ) in gray , representing raw code repositories - Arrow pointing down to question mark symbol , indicating the challenge of conversion - At bottom : Empty or sparse tool icons with dotted outlines , showing limited available MCP services - Text label : " Limited MCP Services Available " ** Center - Code2MCP Solution :** - prominent rightward arrow spanning the divider - Arrow colored in gradient blue -to - green - Text overlay on arrow : " Code2MCP " in bold white font - Subtitle below : " Automated Transformation " - Small gear / cog icons around the arrow to represent the automated process ** Right Side - Consumption ( Current Research Focus ) :** - Background : Light green gradient to indicate the well - researched area - Title at top : " CONSUMPTION ( Well - Researched )" in bold green text - Visual : large brain or AI model icon at the top right - Below it : Multiple organized , colorful tool icons in grid layout representing available MCP services - Icons should include : calculator , microscope , graph , database , API symbol , etc . - Arrows pointing from tools toward the AI model - Text label : " LLM Tool Selection & Usage " - Bottom text : " Existing Research Focus " ** Additional Elements :** - thin vertical dashed line separating the two sides - Small icons representing different domains ( bioinformatics DNA helix , mathematical equations , fluid dynamics waves ) near the GitHub repository - Color scheme : Red / pink for problems , blue / green for solutions , green for established areas - Clean , modern flat design style with subtle shadows ** Text Labels :** - Top center : " MCP Ecosystem Challenge " - Left side bottom : " How to populate the tool pool ?" - Right side bottom : " How to select from existing tools ?" Illustrator prompt with RAG: The prompt writer can simply refer to the visual elements in the reference. So, it can focus more on specifying the content while maintaining aesthetics.
